title: Physical Activity and Exercise for Health
topic: Physical Activity and Exercise for Health

content.overview:
Regular physical activity is one of the most important things people can do for their health. It prevents and manages many non-communicable diseases (NCDs) including heart disease, stroke, type 2 diabetes, several cancers, obesity, depression, and anxiety. Physical inactivity is the fourth leading risk factor for global mortality. WHO estimates that physical inactivity costs the global economy $27 billion annually in healthcare and lost productivity. In India, rapid urbanisation and sedentary lifestyles are driving an NCD epidemic — making physical activity promotion a public health priority.

- Physical inactivity causes approximately 3.2 million deaths globally each year.
- 1 in 4 adults globally is insufficiently active; in India, rates of physical inactivity are rising rapidly.
- Regular physical activity reduces risk of heart disease, stroke, hypertension, diabetes, certain cancers, depression, dementia, and osteoporosis.
- Even small amounts of activity are beneficial — some activity is better than none.
- Sedentary behaviour (prolonged sitting) is an independent risk factor for disease even in people who exercise.
- India's National Programme for Prevention and Control of NCDs (NP-NCD) promotes physical activity.
- Yoga and traditional Indian exercises (yoga, surya namaskar, lathi, mallakhamb) are endorsed by Government of India as culturally appropriate activity.

Q: Is walking enough for good health?
A: Yes, for most people brisk walking (fast enough that you can talk but feel slightly breathless) for 30 minutes most days of the week meets WHO guidelines and provides significant health benefits.

Q: Does yoga count as physical activity?
A: Yes. Yoga improves flexibility, muscle strength, and balance. Active yoga sequences (like surya namaskar, power yoga) also count toward moderate-intensity aerobic activity targets.

Q: Can people with diabetes exercise?
A: Yes — exercise is a cornerstone of diabetes management. It improves blood sugar control significantly. However, check blood sugar before and after exercise and discuss with your doctor about adjustments to medication if needed.

Q: Is exercise safe during pregnancy?
A: For most healthy pregnant women, yes. Regular moderate exercise (like walking, swimming, prenatal yoga) is safe and beneficial. Avoid contact sports, activities with fall risk, and exercising on your back after the first trimester. Consult your doctor.

Q: How much is too much exercise?
A: For most people, more activity (up to a point) provides more benefit. Overtraining syndrome (excessive without adequate rest) is mainly a concern for competitive athletes. Listen to your body and allow adequate rest and recovery.

--- IMPORTANT SAFETY NOTE ---
Physical activity recommendations are for awareness. If you have any medical conditions, consult a doctor or physiotherapist before starting a new exercise regimen. Never ignore chest pain, breathlessness, or dizziness during exercise — stop immediately and seek medical care. Exercise is preventive health — not a substitute for medical treatment.
types: Uses large muscle groups rhythmically; increases heart rate and breathing
Moderate intensity: Can talk but not sing — brisk walking, cycling, swimming, dancing
Vigorous intensity: Cannot say more than a few words without pausing to breathe — running, HIIT, playing sports like football or badminton
Benefits: Heart health, blood sugar control, weight management, lung function
Examples: Bodyweight exercises (push-ups, squats, lunges), weights, resistance bands, yoga
Benefits: Bone density, metabolism, posture, reduces sarcopenia (muscle loss with age)
Benefits: Falls prevention (especially in elderly), joint health, reduces injury risk
Combines physical postures, breathing, and meditation
WHO recognises yoga as beneficial physical activity
International Yoga Day: June 21 (initiated by India)
Government of India Ministry of AYUSH promotes yoga as preventive health
Not separately specified or not applicable in the available source text for this topic.
symptoms: Not separately specified or not applicable in the available source text for this topic.
warning_signs: Not separately specified or not applicable in the available source text for this topic.
risk_factors: Doubled risk of cardiovascular disease and diabetes
Increased risk of colon and breast cancer
Higher rates of depression and anxiety
Muscle wasting (sarcopenia) — particularly dangerous in elderly
Prolonged sitting is harmful even if you exercise at other times
Increases risk of type 2 diabetes, cardiovascular disease, and metabolic syndrome
Break sitting time: Stand up, stretch, or walk for a few minutes every 30–60 minutes
Especially relevant for office workers, students, and drivers
Not separately specified or not applicable in the available source text for this topic.
prevention: FOR CHILDREN (5–17 years):
At least 60 minutes of moderate-to-vigorous intensity physical activity daily
Include vigorous intensity activity at least 3 days per week
Include bone-strengthening and muscle-strengthening activities at least 3 days per week
Limit screen time; reduce prolonged sitting
Examples: Running, cycling, swimming, playing cricket, football, skipping, yoga, dance
FOR ADULTS (18–64 years):
At least 150–300 minutes of moderate-intensity aerobic activity per week, OR
At least 75–150 minutes of vigorous-intensity aerobic activity per week, OR
Equivalent combination of moderate and vigorous activity
Muscle-strengthening activities (resistance exercises) at least 2 days per week
Reduce sedentary time; any physical activity is better than none
FOR OLDER ADULTS (65+ years):
Same as adults (150–300 min moderate or 75–150 min vigorous weekly)
Add balance and coordination activities to prevent falls (3+ days/week)
Those unable to meet recommendations should be as physically active as their abilities allow
Examples: Walking, chair exercises, gentle yoga, swimming, dancing
FOR PREGNANT WOMEN:
At least 150 minutes of moderate-intensity aerobic activity per week throughout pregnancy and postpartum (if no contraindications)
Strengthening exercises beneficial; avoid lying flat on back after first trimester
Consult a doctor before starting a new exercise programme during pregnancy
Physical health:
Reduces risk of cardiovascular disease by 35%
Reduces risk of type 2 diabetes by up to 50%
Reduces risk of colon cancer by 30%, breast cancer by 20%
Controls blood pressure (reduces systolic by 4–9 mmHg)
Maintains healthy weight; improves insulin sensitivity
Strengthens bones and muscles; prevents osteoporosis
Improves sleep quality
Mental health:
Reduces symptoms of depression and anxiety as effectively as some medications
Improves mood, self-esteem, and cognitive function
Reduces risk of dementia and Alzheimer's disease by 30–35%
Reduces stress hormones (cortisol, adrenaline); increases endorphins
Starting out:
Start slowly — even 10 minutes of walking daily has benefits; gradually increase
Choose activities you enjoy (more likely to continue)
Break activity into smaller sessions (3 × 10 minutes = 30 minutes)
Use stairs instead of lifts; walk short distances instead of taking vehicles
Get a physical activity buddy / join a group
Practical India-specific tips:
Morning or evening walks in a park (most cities have public parks)
Yoga at community yoga centres or with YouTube resources
Cycling for short commutes
Playing cricket, kabaddi, badminton, or kho-kho
Use local PT periods/sports grounds if in school or college
Exercise outdoors early morning or after sunset to avoid peak heat in summer
Barriers and solutions:
Barrier: No time → Solution: Integrate activity into daily routine (walk during lunch, cycle to work)
Barrier: No gym/money → Solution: Bodyweight exercises at home; parks are free; yoga mats are inexpensive
Barrier: Health conditions → Solution: Even light activity is beneficial; consult doctor for personalised guidance
Barrier: Extreme heat (India) → Solution: Exercise early morning, late evening, or indoors; stay hydrated
Stay hydrated — especially important in India's hot climate
Wear appropriate footwear to prevent joint and foot injuries
Warm up before and cool down after exercise
Start slowly if returning after illness or long break
If you have heart disease, diabetes, hypertension, or joint problems — consult a doctor before starting vigorous exercise
Listen to your body: chest pain, dizziness, or severe breathlessness during exercise = STOP and seek medical help
Not separately specified or not applicable in the available source text for this topic.
environmental_control: India's Smart Cities Mission includes walkable footpaths, cycling tracks, open gyms
Fit India Movement: Government of India initiative to promote physical activity and sports
Khelo India: Promotes sports from grassroots level for youth
Pradhan Mantri Jan Arogya Yojana: Some states include wellness programmes
vaccination: Not separately specified or not applicable in the available source text for this topic.
diagnosis: Not separately specified or not applicable in the available source text for this topic.
treatment_awareness_only: Treatment details are for awareness only. Consult a qualified healthcare professional.
when_to_see_a_doctor: Before starting vigorous exercise if you:
Are over 40 and have not exercised for a long time
Have been diagnosed with heart disease, diabetes, hypertension, or asthma
Have joint pain (knees, hips, back) that limits mobility
During or after exercise — seek immediate care if:
Severe shortness of breath out of proportion to effort
india_specific_context: Fit India Movement (2019): PM Modi's initiative; Fit India app; Fit India School Week
International Day of Yoga: June 21 — India-led UN resolution; mass events nationwide
NP-NCD: National Programme for Prevention and Control of NCDs — includes physical activity counselling at health centres
ICMR Dietary Guidelines for Indians (2024) recommend 45–60 minutes of moderate physical activity daily
School physical education: Compulsory PE periods in schools; CBSE promotes sports
Open-air gyms (Jan Jeevan gyms) installed in parks across major cities
Air quality consideration: On high-pollution days (AQI >200) in cities like Delhi, avoid vigorous outdoor exercise
For lifestyle disease counselling at Ayushman Bharat HWCs, contact your nearest PHC or call 104. Yoga and wellness programmes: ayush.gov.in.
